name: azure-identity-dotnet
description: A skill that provides guidance on using the Azure.Identity library for .NET applications. Learn how to authenticate to Azure services using managed identities, service principals, and user credentials with best practices for secure token acquisition.
capabilities: Explain the different credential types in Azure.Identity (DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, ClientSecretCredential)
Help configure authentication for Azure App Service, Azure Functions, and containerized applications
Provide code examples for acquiring tokens to access Azure resources
Guide on handling authentication errors and implementing retry policies
Explain best practices for storing and managing Azure credentials securely
Assist with integrating Azure AD authentication into ASP.NET Core applications
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill providing guidance on the Azure.Identity .NET library. Static analysis found no executable code, no suspicious patterns, and no security issues. The skill is purely informational about Microsoft's official Azure SDK authentication library.
valueStatement: Authenticating to Azure services requires understanding complex token flows and credential types. This skill provides clear guidance on using Azure.Identity to implement secure authentication with managed identities, service principals, and user credentials in .NET applications.
limitations: This skill provides guidance but does not execute Azure operations
Cannot create or manage Azure AD applications or service principals
Does not provide debugging for specific Azure subscription permissions issues
Does not replace understanding of Azure RBAC role assignments

useCases.targetUser: .NET developers working with Azure cloud services
useCases.title: Cloud Developer Implementing Azure Authentication
useCases.description: A .NET developer building applications that need to authenticate to Azure services like Key Vault, Blob Storage, or Cosmos DB

useCases.title: DevOps Engineer Configuring Managed Identity
useCases.description: A DevOps engineer setting up authentication for applications running on Azure App Service or Azure Kubernetes Service

useCases.title: Security Engineer Reviewing Authentication Code
useCases.description: A security professional reviewing .NET code for proper Azure authentication patterns and credential handling

promptTemplates.title: Getting Started with Azure.Identity
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I authenticate to Azure services using the Azure.Identity library in my .NET application? Explain the different credential options and when to use each one.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner asking about basic authentication setup

promptTemplates.title: Using Managed Identity
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to configure and use Managed Identity authentication for an ASP.NET Core application running on Azure App Service.
promptTemplates.scenario: Developer wanting to use Azure managed identities

promptTemplates.title: Service Principal Authentication
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I authenticate using a service principal with ClientSecretCredential in a production .NET application? Include best practices for storing credentials securely.

promptTemplates.title: Advanced Token Caching
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement token caching for Azure.Identity in a high-scale .NET application. Show how to reduce authentication calls and handle token refresh properly.

bestPractices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for simplest setup that works across development and production environments
Always use Managed Identity in production on Azure to avoid storing secrets in code or environment variables
Implement proper error handling for authentication failures with retry logic for transient errors
antiPatterns: Storing Azure client secrets in source code or configuration files that are committed to version control
Using the same service principal for multiple applications without proper scoping
Ignoring token expiration and not implementing proper token caching or refresh logic

faq.question: What is Azure.Identity?
faq.answer: Azure.Identity is a Microsoft library for .NET that provides authentication abstractions for Azure services. It simplifies acquiring tokens from Azure AD for accessing Azure resources.

faq.question: What is the difference between DefaultAzureCredential and ManagedIdentityCredential?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential tries multiple authentication methods automatically. ManagedIdentityCredential specifically uses the system-assigned or user-assigned identity of the Azure resource where your code runs.

faq.question: Can I use Azure.Identity for on-premises applications?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use ClientSecretCredential or InteractiveBrowserCredential for on-premises apps, but managed identity only works on Azure resources.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication errors in Azure.Identity?
faq.answer: Catch AuthenticationFailedException for invalid credentials, and RequestFailedException for Azure resource access issues. Implement retry logic with ExponentialBackoff for transient failures.

faq.question: Is Azure.Identity the same as Azure AD?
faq.answer: No, Azure AD (now Microsoft Entra ID) is the identity provider. Azure.Identity is the client library that simplifies authenticating with Azure AD to get access tokens.

faq.question: Does Azure.Identity support token caching?
faq.answer: Yes, Azure.Identity includes built-in token caching. For advanced scenarios, you can implement custom token caching using TokenCacheCredential or use Microsoft.Identity.Web for more complex scenarios.
